effects.title: Holy Grail Layout
effects.summary: Header, footer, fluid main content, and two fixed sidebars — the classic app shell, in CSS Grid.
effects.description: The famously-hard-in-the-old-days 'holy grail' layout (sticky header/footer, flexible center, fixed-width side rails) expressed cleanly with grid-template-areas.
effects.performance_notes: Pure layout; free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use landmark elements (header/nav/main/aside/footer). Keep DOM order sensible (main content early) for screen readers even if visually centered.
variations: Collapse rails to a single column on mobile
Replace a rail with a collapsible drawer
effects.ai_usage: The default app/dashboard/blog shell. Use grid-template-areas to name header/nav/main/aside/footer regions and grid-template-columns/rows to size them (fixed rails + 1fr center). Assign each landmark element to its area. On mobile, redefine the areas to stack into one column inside a media/container query. Keep semantic landmarks for accessibility.

effects.title: Masonry Grid (CSS columns)
effects.summary: A Pinterest-style staggered masonry layout using CSS multi-columns — no JS.
effects.description: Uses the CSS columns property with break-inside: avoid to flow items into balanced vertical columns of varying heights, the simplest dependency-free masonry.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (CSS columns). Note: items flow top-to-bottom THEN across, so reading order differs from Grid.
effects.performance_notes: Pure CSS; free. (Native CSS Grid masonry is emerging but not yet widely shipped.)
effects.accessibility_notes: Column flow means visual order is column-by-column, which can differ from DOM order expectations — fine for galleries, less ideal for sequential content.
variations: Native grid masonry (grid-template-rows: masonry) where supported
JS libraries for exact left-to-right order
effects.ai_usage: Use for image galleries and pin boards where item heights vary and exact order doesn't matter. Set columns: column-width Npx (fluid) or N (fixed) on the container, column-gap for gutters, and break-inside: avoid + margin-bottom on each item. Caveat: items fill top-to-bottom per column, so it's not left-to-right reading order — use a JS lib or future native grid masonry if order matters.

effects.title: Responsive Card Grid (no media queries)
effects.summary: A card grid that reflows columns automatically using auto-fit + minmax — zero breakpoints.
effects.description: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(MIN, 1fr)) makes the grid add/remove columns based on available width, the single most useful responsive grid one-liner.
effects.performance_notes: Pure layout; free. No JS or media queries.
effects.accessibility_notes: DOM order = visual order, so tab/reading order is correct by default.

customization.name: min column width
customization.description: The minmax minimum that decides when columns wrap

variations: auto-fill (keeps empty tracks) vs auto-fit (collapses them)
Add minmax(0,1fr) to fix overflow with long content
effects.ai_usage: The default responsive grid for card lists, galleries, product grids. One line: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(MIN, 1fr)). Choose MIN = the smallest acceptable card width; the grid computes column count per viewport with no media queries. Use auto-fit to collapse empty tracks (cards stretch), auto-fill to keep them. Add minmax(0,1fr) if long words overflow. Resize the frame to watch it reflow.

effects.title: Split-Screen Layout
effects.summary: A 50/50 split: a fixed visual panel on one side, scrollable content on the other — collapsing to stacked on mobile.
effects.description: The split-screen archetype: a full-height visual (image/brand) pinned on one half while the other half holds scrollable content. Common for auth screens, product intros, and editorial. Includes the responsive collapse and a tasteful sticky behavior.
effects.performance_notes: Pure CSS Grid + sticky. No JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Source order = reading order (content first is fine); landmarks used; the visual panel is decorative. Stacks to a single column at small widths so nothing is cut off.
variations: Visual on the left for auth
effects.ai_usage: Use for auth pages, product intros, and editorial layouts. Two-column grid: one half a full-height visual (sticky if the other side scrolls), the other half content. Keep DOM order sensible for reading/SR, make the visual decorative, and collapse to one stacked column on small screens. Adjust the ratio via grid-template-columns.
effects.source:
◑ Split-screen
archetype

Step 01

Pinned visual, scrolling story

The left panel stays put while this side scrolls — ideal for guiding attention through a narrative.

Step 02

Great for auth & onboarding

Brand or imagery on one side, the form or content on the other. Calm and focused.

effects.title: Bouncing Dots
effects.summary: Three dots bounce in sequence — the friendly 'typing…' / loading indicator.
effects.description: Three dots share one bounce keyframe with staggered animation-delays so they rise and fall in a wave, evoking chat 'typing' indicators and lightweight loaders.
effects.performance_notes: Three nodes animating translateY — trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Wrap with role=status + a label. Under prefers-reduced-motion, replace bounce with a gentle opacity pulse.
variations: More dots for a longer wave
effects.ai_usage: Use for chat 'typing' states and small inline loaders. Three .dot spans share a bounce animation with delays of 0, 0.16s, 0.32s. Tune --dot color and size. For reduced motion, swap the translateY bounce for an opacity pulse so it still signals activity without movement.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Circular Progress Ring
effects.summary: A determinate SVG ring that fills to a percentage via stroke-dashoffset, with a live label.
effects.description: An SVG circle whose stroke-dasharray equals its circumference; animating stroke-dashoffset reveals the arc proportional to a percentage — the standard circular progress meter.
effects.performance_notes: Animating stroke-dashoffset is cheap. For pure decoration use CSS; for real values update from JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Add role=progressbar with aria-valuenow/min/max and keep the numeric label as text so the value is announced. Not just color — the number conveys the state.
effects.ai_usage: Use for upload/download progress, scores, and timers. circumference = 2*pi*r; set the circle's stroke-dasharray to that, and stroke-dashoffset = circumference * (1 - pct). Rotate the circle -90deg so it starts at top. Update offset + the text label + aria-valuenow from JS as progress changes. The demo animates 0→100% on a loop.
effects.source: 0%

effects.title: Dual Ring / Orbit Spinner
effects.summary: Two concentric arcs spin in opposite directions for a more dynamic loader.
effects.description: A container and a pseudo-element each render an arc (colored border on opposing sides) and counter-rotate, producing an orbiting double-ring spinner.
effects.performance_notes: Two rotating layers, transform-only — cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Pair with role=status + label. Slow down under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.ai_usage: A slightly fancier spinner for splash/loading screens. Outer element has border-top-color:--c1 and spins one way; ::after is an inset ring with border-bottom-color:--c2 spinning the other way. Keep within role=status and a hidden label. Size via --size.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Indeterminate Progress Bar
effects.summary: A sliding accent segment loops across a track when progress can't be measured.
effects.description: A track contains a short bar that animates its position/width across, signaling ongoing work of unknown duration — the top-of-page loading bar pattern.
effects.performance_notes: Animates transform/left of one segment; cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use role="progressbar" with aria-valuetext or an adjacent 'Loading' label; omit aria-valuenow when indeterminate. Animation continues under reduced-motion but you may slow it.
variations: Fix to top of viewport for route changes
effects.ai_usage: Use for unknown-duration tasks and page/route transitions (often pinned to the top of the viewport). The track is overflow:hidden; an inner segment animates from left:-40% to left:100%. Switch to a real determinate <progress> or a width-driven bar once you know the percentage. Set role=progressbar and a label.

effects.title: Orbit Loader
effects.summary: A nucleus with electrons orbiting on tilted rings at different speeds — an atomic, premium loading indicator.
effects.description: Concentric rings rotate at different rates, each carrying a glowing dot, around a central pulsing core — like an atom. A more characterful loader than a plain spinner, good for science/AI/dev brands.
effects.performance_notes: Three rotation animations + one pulse, transform/opacity only — GPU-friendly. Pure CSS, no JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Wrap with role='status' and a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label so state is announced. Under prefers-reduced-motion, stop rotation (show a static atom).
variations: Single ring for a minimal look
effects.ai_usage: Use as a branded loading state for science/AI/dev products. Stack rings (each a bordered ellipse rotated in 3D) with a dot positioned on the edge; rotate each ring at a different --speed and direction; pulse the core. Wrap in role='status' + a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label and freeze rotation under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Ring Spinner
effects.summary: The classic spinning ring with a single colored arc, drawn with one element.
effects.description: A circular element with a transparent border on three sides and a colored top border spins continuously — the most recognizable loading indicator.
effects.performance_notes: Animating rotation via transform is GPU-friendly. One DOM node.
effects.accessibility_notes: Add role="status" and an aria-label/visually-hidden 'Loading' for screen readers. The spin itself is essential feedback, but pair with a text label.
variations: Two arcs (top+bottom) for a denser look
Dashed border for a tick ring
Slow spin for a calmer feel
effects.ai_usage: Default spinner for async actions and page/section loads. Apply .spinner; the colored border-top-color + transparent other sides creates the arc, animation:spin 1s linear infinite rotates it. Always wrap with role=status and a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label. Size via --size; match --color to brand.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Skeleton Loader
effects.summary: Gray placeholder blocks with a sweeping shimmer that mimic content while it loads.
effects.description: Content-shaped gray boxes get a moving highlight gradient (the same trick as shimmer text) to signal that real content is on its way — reducing perceived load time.
effects.performance_notes: Animates background-position only; cheap even for many blocks.
effects.accessibility_notes: Mark the region aria-busy="true" and/or role=status so assistive tech announces loading. Shimmer stops under prefers-reduced-motion (static gray).
variations: Pulse opacity instead of shimmer sweep
effects.ai_usage: Show in place of content during data fetches; match block shapes/sizes to the real layout to avoid reflow jank. Apply .skeleton to each placeholder; it tiles a 3-stop gradient (base, sheen, base) at background-size:200% and animates background-position. Set aria-busy on the container, then swap in real content when loaded.

effects.title: Wave Bars Loader
effects.summary: A row of bars that rise and fall in a travelling wave — an audio-equalizer-style loading / 'now playing' indicator.
effects.description: Several bars scale on the Y axis with staggered delays so a wave ripples across them. Doubles as a music 'now playing' indicator and a lightweight loading state.
effects.performance_notes: One keyframe reused with per-bar animation-delay; transform: scaleY only — GPU-friendly. Pure CSS.
effects.accessibility_notes: As a loader, wrap with role='status' + a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label; as a decorative 'now playing' indicator, mark aria-hidden. Stops to a static row under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: count / --speed
customization.description: Number of bars and cycle time

variations: Mirror vertically for a symmetric EQ
effects.ai_usage: Use as a loading indicator or a music 'now playing' badge. Render N bars; animate transform: scaleY with one keyframe and stagger each bar's animation-delay so a wave travels across. For loading, wrap in role='status' with a hidden label; for decoration, aria-hide. Freeze to a static row under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Easing Curve Showcase
effects.summary: Side-by-side comparison of common easing curves, animated, with their cubic-bezier tokens.
effects.description: A reference specimen running the same movement through several named easings (ease-out, ease-in-out, a snappy back-ease, etc.) so you can feel the difference and copy the cubic-bezier values as motion tokens.
effects.performance_notes: A handful of transform loops; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Demonstration motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion. In real UIs, prefer ease-out for entrances and keep durations short.
variations: Pair each ease with a recommended duration
effects.ai_usage: A motion-token reference. Store these as --ease-out, --ease-in-out, --ease-emphasized, --ease-back tokens and reference them in transitions/animations for consistency. Rules of thumb: ease-out for elements entering/responding to input (fast start, gentle settle), ease-in for exits, ease-in-out for movement between two on-screen states, linear ONLY for continuous motion (spinners). Avoid the default 'ease' for everything. Always pair with a reduced-motion guard.
effects.source:
--ease-out
--ease-in-out
--ease-emphasized
--ease-back

effects.title: Motion Duration Scale
effects.summary: A small set of duration tokens (instant→slow) mapped to UI scenarios, demonstrated live.
effects.description: Defines --dur-fast/base/slow tokens and shows the same animation at each speed, with guidance on which to use where — the timing half of a motion system.
effects.performance_notes: Trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep UI transitions short (<300ms) to feel responsive; long durations frustrate. All motion respects prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Pair each duration with a default easing
effects.ai_usage: The timing half of a motion system. Store --dur-fast (~120ms, hovers/toggles/micro-feedback), --dur-base (~240ms, most transitions, dropdowns), --dur-slow (~400ms, larger surfaces/modals/page-level). Larger/farther-moving elements get longer durations; small UI feedback stays fast. Reference tokens in all transitions for consistency, and combine with the easing tokens. Keep everything under ~400ms for UI.
effects.source:
--dur-fast 120ms
--dur-base 240ms
--dur-slow 400ms

fast = hovers/toggles · base = dropdowns/most transitions · slow = modals/page-level

effects.title: Stagger Choreography
effects.summary: A list whose items animate in one after another with an incremental delay.
effects.description: Demonstrates staggering: the same entrance animation applied to siblings with animation-delay derived from each item's index, so they cascade rather than appear at once — a core motion-design principle.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity per item; cheap. The delay is set via an --i custom property * a step.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep the total stagger short so content isn't withheld too long. Disable (show all at once) under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: --i
customization.description: Item index (set inline or via nth-child)

variations: Stagger by distance from a focal point
Combine with scroll-reveal so it triggers on entry
effects.ai_usage: Use whenever multiple siblings enter together (menus, lists, grids) — staggering reads as intentional and guides the eye. Apply one entrance animation to all items and set animation-delay: calc(var(--i) * var(--step)) where --i is the item index (inline style or :nth-child). Keep --step small (50-100ms) and the total under ~600ms. Combine with IntersectionObserver to trigger on scroll. Disable under reduced motion. The demo replays on a loop.

effects.title: Animated Dropdown Menu
effects.summary: A dropdown that fades and slides down from its trigger, toggled accessibly on click.
effects.description: A button toggles a menu that animates from a slightly-raised, transparent state into place. Click-outside and Escape close it; aria-expanded is kept in sync.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transition on a small element; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Trigger is a button with aria-haspopup + aria-expanded; menu items are focusable; Escape closes and returns focus to the trigger; clicking outside closes. For full menu semantics add role=menu/menuitem and arrow-key navigation.
effects.ai_usage: Use for account/overflow menus and selects. Toggle a hidden menu from a button; sync aria-expanded and animate opacity + translateY for the open transition (use visibility/display to keep it out of the tab order when closed). MUST handle: click-outside to close, Escape to close + refocus the trigger, and keyboard access to items. Add role=menu/menuitem and arrow navigation for a true menu widget.
effects.source: ProfileBillingSettingsSign out

effects.title: Animated Underline Links
effects.summary: Nav links whose underline grows from the center (or slides in) on hover/focus.
effects.description: Uses a scaleX-animated pseudo-element underline with transform-origin so it grows from center, plus a left-to-right slide variant — the cleanest way to animate underlines.
effects.performance_notes: Transform-only on a pseudo-element — GPU-friendly.
effects.accessibility_notes: Apply the same effect on :focus-visible, not just :hover, so keyboard users see it. Keep an accessible color/contrast for the link text itself.
effects.ai_usage: Standard nav/link hover. Link is position:relative; a ::after is an absolutely-positioned 2px bar at the bottom, scaleX(0) with transform-origin:center, transitioning to scaleX(1) on hover AND focus-visible. Change transform-origin to left for a directional slide. Always include :focus-visible for keyboard accessibility.
effects.source: Home Work About Contact

effects.title: Command Palette (⌘K)
effects.summary: A ⌘K / Ctrl+K spotlight that opens a searchable command menu — fuzzy filter, arrow-key navigation, Enter to run, Esc to close.
effects.description: The keyboard-first navigation pattern popularized by Linear, Raycast, and VS Code. Press ⌘K (or click) to open a centered overlay with a search field that filters a command list live; arrow keys move the selection, Enter activates, Escape closes.
effects.performance_notes: In-memory filter over a small list; no libraries. Opens/closes by toggling a class.
effects.accessibility_notes: Dialog uses role='dialog' aria-modal; input is labeled; the list is role='listbox' with aria-selected on the active option and aria-activedescendant on the input; Esc closes and focus returns to the trigger. Provide a visible trigger too (not keyboard-only).
effects.ai_usage: Use as primary navigation/search in apps and dev tools. Toggle a dialog on ⌘K/Ctrl+K AND a visible button; filter the command array on input; track a selected index for ArrowUp/Down + Enter; Esc closes and restores focus to the trigger. Wire ARIA: role='dialog' aria-modal, input with aria-activedescendant, listbox/options with aria-selected. Always offer a clickable trigger, not just the shortcut.

effects.title: Hamburger → X Morph
effects.summary: A three-line menu icon that smoothly morphs into a close (X) when toggled.
effects.description: Three bars transform on toggle: the middle fades, the top and bottom rotate ±45° and translate to form an X — the canonical menu-button animation, on a real accessible button.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transitions on three spans; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Built on a <button> with aria-expanded and aria-label that updates on toggle — essential for screen readers. Transition removed under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Morph to back-arrow instead of X
Spin the whole icon while morphing
effects.ai_usage: Mobile nav toggle. MUST be a real <button> with aria-label and aria-expanded toggled in JS (also drive opening the actual menu from the same state). Three .bar spans; on the .open state, translate+rotate the top/bottom into an X and fade the middle. The top/bottom translate distance equals the gap between bars. Remove transition under reduced motion.

effects.title: Mega Menu
effects.summary: A full-width dropdown panel with multiple columns of grouped links and a featured promo — the navigation for content-heavy sites.
effects.description: Hovering (or focusing) a top-level nav item opens a wide panel organized into titled columns, plus a highlighted feature card. Reveals smoothly and is keyboard-accessible — the standard pattern for large product/marketing sites.
effects.performance_notes: Opacity/transform reveal on a single panel; a tiny JS toggle for focus/escape. No libraries.
effects.accessibility_notes: Trigger is a real button with aria-expanded; opens on hover AND keyboard focus; Escape closes; links are reachable in order. Panel hidden from AT when closed.
variations: Multiple mega-menu triggers in a bar
effects.ai_usage: Use for content-heavy product/marketing headers. A nav button with aria-expanded toggles a full-width panel of titled link columns + a feature card; open on hover and on focus-within, close on Escape and outside click. Keep links in logical DOM order, hide the panel from assistive tech when closed, and ensure it's fully keyboard-operable (not hover-only).

effects.title: Sliding Tab Indicator
effects.summary: A pill/underline indicator that glides to the active tab.
effects.description: A single absolutely-positioned indicator is moved (translate + width) to match the selected tab, giving the smooth 'shared element' tab transition instead of a hard jump.
effects.performance_notes: Animates transform/width of one element; cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use role=tablist/tab/tabpanel and manage aria-selected + roving tabindex; the moving pill is decorative (visual only). Keyboard arrow navigation should move focus between tabs.
variations: Auto-detect active on load from aria-selected
effects.ai_usage: Use for segmented controls and tab bars. Render real tab buttons in a tablist; keep ONE indicator element and on tab change set its transform: translateX(offsetLeft) and width to the active tab's metrics (read from getBoundingClientRect/offsetLeft). Drive selection with proper ARIA (role=tab, aria-selected, arrow-key roving focus); the pill is purely visual. Recompute on resize.

effects.title: Blink Text
effects.summary: The infamous <blink> effect, recreated accessibly with a gentle opacity animation.
effects.description: Reproduces blinking text with a CSS keyframe, but uses a slower fade and a hard stop under reduced-motion instead of the harsh original — a tasteful homage to the most reviled HTML tag.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (CSS version). The <blink> tag is long removed from browsers.
effects.performance_notes: Single opacity animation; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Rapid flashing is a serious WCAG/seizure risk. Keep any blink SLOW (>1s), never use on essential content, and disable entirely under prefers-reduced-motion (handled here). Honestly: prefer not to use blinking at all outside of nostalgia pieces.
variations: Soft fade rather than hard on/off
effects.ai_usage: Nostalgia/retro pieces only. Implement as a slow opacity keyframe (step-end for hard blink, ease for soft), period >= 1s, and ALWAYS disable under prefers-reduced-motion. Never apply to important or interactive content — flashing is an accessibility hazard. Treat this as a deliberate retro flourish, not a UI pattern.
effects.source: UNDER CONSTRUCTION

effects.title: CSS Marquee
effects.summary: A modern, accessible replacement for the deprecated <marquee> tag using CSS animation.
effects.description: Recreates the scrolling-ticker look of the old <marquee> element with a translateX keyframe, pausing on hover and under reduced-motion — without the deprecated tag.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (the CSS version). The original <marquee> tag is deprecated — do not use it.
effects.performance_notes: Single translateX loop; cheap. Duplicate the content for a seamless infinite ticker.
effects.accessibility_notes: Auto-scrolling text is a WCAG concern: provide a pause control (this pauses on hover/focus) and STOP entirely under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep the full text in the DOM for screen readers.
effects.ai_usage: Use for news tickers, stock tapes, and retro homages — never the deprecated <marquee> tag. Wrap content in an overflow:hidden track; the inner element animates translateX(0 → -100%). Duplicate the content inline for a seamless loop. PROVIDE A PAUSE (hover/focus + a real button in production) and disable under prefers-reduced-motion for accessibility.
effects.source: ★ Welcome to my homepage ★ Best viewed in 800x600 ★ Sign my guestbook! ★

effects.title: Rainbow Cycling Text
effects.summary: Text that cycles through the rainbow via an animated hue rotation — peak GeoCities energy.
effects.description: Fills text with a rainbow gradient (background-clip) and animates the hue with a filter or moving gradient, recreating the animated rainbow text of the early personal web.
effects.performance_notes: Animating hue-rotate filter or background-position; cheap for a heading.
effects.accessibility_notes: Rainbow text can be low-contrast and hard to read; keep it to large display headings, not body copy. Animation stops under prefers-reduced-motion (static rainbow remains).
variations: Pair with a comic-sans retro vibe
effects.ai_usage: Retro/playful headings and easter eggs. Apply a rainbow linear-gradient clipped to text, then animate filter: hue-rotate(0 → 360deg) for the cycling. Keep to large text for readability and disable animation under reduced motion. For a per-letter wave, wrap letters in spans and stagger animation-delay.
effects.source: ~ Welcome ~

effects.title: Starfield Warp
effects.summary: Stars streaking toward the viewer — the screensaver/space-warp staple, on canvas.
effects.description: A canvas animation projects randomized 3D star points toward the camera and resets them when they pass, producing the classic flying-through-space starfield.
effects.performance_notes: Canvas with a few hundred points at 60fps is light. Cap star count and use a single clear per frame. Stop the rAF loop when offscreen in production.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative full-screen motion; halt the loop and show a static frame under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep foreground text on a contrasted surface.
variations: Slower drift for an ambient sky
effects.ai_usage: Hero/space-themed backgrounds and intros. Canvas sized to the container; each star has x,y in [-1,1] and z depth; per frame decrease z (move toward camera), project to screen as x/z, y/z, draw a dot whose size grows as z shrinks; respawn at far z when it passes. Use canvas (not DOM) for hundreds of points. Stop the loop / show static under reduced motion and when offscreen.
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>Starfield Warp</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "starfield",
    "title": "Starfield Warp",
    "summary": "Stars streaking toward the viewer — the screensaver/space-warp staple, on canvas.",
    "description": "A canvas animation projects randomized 3D star points toward the camera and resets them when they pass, producing the classic flying-through-space starfield.",
    "theme": "retro-web",
    "categories": ["retro", "canvas", "background"],
    "tags": ["starfield", "warp", "canvas", "space", "screensaver", "particles"],
    "tech": ["canvas", "js"],
    "era": "2000s",
    "difficulty": "advanced",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers (canvas 2D).",
    "performance_notes": "Canvas with a few hundred points at 60fps is light. Cap star count and use a single clear per frame. Stop the rAF loop when offscreen in production.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative full-screen motion; halt the loop and show a static frame under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep foreground text on a contrasted surface.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "STAR_COUNT", "description": "Number of stars", "default": "400" },
      { "name": "SPEED", "description": "Warp speed", "default": "0.02" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Colored/hyperspace streaks (draw trails)", "Slower drift for an ambient sky", "Mouse-steerable camera"],
    "related": ["floating-particles"],
    "ai_usage": "Hero/space-themed backgrounds and intros. Canvas sized to the container; each star has x,y in [-1,1] and z depth; per frame decrease z (move toward camera), project to screen as x/z, y/z, draw a dot whose size grows as z shrinks; respawn at far z when it passes. Use canvas (not DOM) for hundreds of points. Stop the loop / show static under reduced motion and when offscreen."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; overflow: hidden; background: #000; }
    canvas { display: block; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <canvas id="c"></canvas>
  <script>
    var c = document.getElementById("c"), ctx = c.getContext("2d");
    var STAR_COUNT = 400, SPEED = 0.02, stars = [];
    var reduce = matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches;
    function resize() { c.width = innerWidth; c.height = innerHeight; }
    resize(); addEventListener("resize", resize);
    function spawn() { return { x: Math.random() * 2 - 1, y: Math.random() * 2 - 1, z: Math.random() }; }
    for (var i = 0; i < STAR_COUNT; i++) stars.push(spawn());
    function draw() {
      ctx.fillStyle = "#000"; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, c.width, c.height);
      var cx = c.width / 2, cy = c.height / 2;
      for (var i = 0; i < stars.length; i++) {
        var s = stars[i];
        if (!reduce) { s.z -= SPEED; if (s.z <= 0.01) stars[i] = spawn(); }
        var sx = cx + (s.x / s.z) * cx, sy = cy + (s.y / s.z) * cy;
        var r = (1 - s.z) * 2.5;
        ctx.fillStyle = "rgba(255,255,255," + (1 - s.z) + ")";
        ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(sx, sy, r, 0, Math.PI * 2); ctx.fill();
      }
      if (!reduce) requestAnimationFrame(draw);
    }
    draw();
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: Image Clip Reveal on Scroll
effects.summary: An image unveils behind a clip-path wipe as it scrolls into view.
effects.description: Combines IntersectionObserver with a clip-path inset transition so the image is wiped into view (rather than just faded) when it enters the viewport — a premium editorial reveal.
effects.performance_notes: Animating clip-path is GPU-friendly; the observer fires once per element.
effects.accessibility_notes: Image is in the DOM regardless; under prefers-reduced-motion show it immediately (no clip). Provide real alt text.
variations: Combine with a slight scale for depth
effects.ai_usage: Use for figure/image reveals in articles and case studies. Start the image clipped (clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0)) and transition to inset(0) when an IntersectionObserver adds the .in class. Change which inset side is 100% to set wipe direction. Short-circuit to fully revealed under prefers-reduced-motion. The demo auto-scrolls so the wipe is visible in the catalog frame.

effects.title: Parallax Layers
effects.summary: Background layers scroll slower than foreground content for a depth illusion — pure CSS.
effects.description: Uses CSS 3D transforms (perspective + translateZ + scale) on a scroll container so deeper layers move slower, achieving parallax without JS scroll listeners.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (the perspective-scroll technique). transform-style support required.
effects.performance_notes: Compositor-driven; far smoother than JS scroll parallax. Watch out for interfering with position:fixed children.
effects.accessibility_notes: Parallax can cause motion discomfort — collapse to a flat scroll under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: translateZ depth
customization.description: Negative Z + scale per layer controls parallax strength

variations: JS variant with rAF for fine control
effects.ai_usage: Hero/storytelling sections with depth. The container gets perspective + overflow scroll; layers are positioned and pushed back with translateZ(negative) then scaled up to refill the viewport — deeper (more negative Z) = slower scroll. Keep the readable content at Z:0. Always provide a reduced-motion flat fallback (remove transforms). The demo auto-scrolls so parallax shows in the frame.
effects.source:
Parallax
Keep scrolling…

effects.title: Scroll Progress Bar
effects.summary: A top bar that fills as the reader scrolls, using pure-CSS scroll-driven animation.
effects.description: Uses the modern animation-timeline: scroll() to scale a fixed bar from 0 to 1 across the page scroll — no JS. Includes a JS fallback note for older browsers.
effects.browser_support: Chromium 115+ and modern browsers support scroll-driven animations; older browsers ignore the timeline (bar stays empty) — provide a tiny JS fallback in production.
effects.performance_notes: Scroll-driven animations run off the main thread — smoother and cheaper than scroll-event JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative reading indicator; safe under reduced-motion (it tracks position, not autonomous motion). Don't convey essential info with it alone.
effects.ai_usage: Reading progress for articles/long pages. Preferred: a fixed bar with transform-origin:left, animation: grow linear, animation-timeline: scroll(root block) — pure CSS, no JS. For browser support, add a JS fallback that sets scaleX = scrollTop / (scrollHeight - innerHeight) on scroll. The demo scrolls its own inner container so it animates in the catalog frame.

effects.title: Scroll Snap Gallery
effects.summary: A horizontal gallery where each slide snaps cleanly into place — pure CSS scroll-snap.
effects.description: Uses scroll-snap-type on a horizontally-scrolling container and scroll-snap-align on children so swiping/scrolling locks each slide to the start, a native carousel with no JS.
effects.performance_notes: Native scrolling; very smooth and JS-free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep slides keyboard-scrollable and focusable; native scroll handles touch/trackpad. For a true carousel add labels and optional prev/next buttons. Snap doesn't trap focus.
effects.ai_usage: Use for image carousels, onboarding slides, and full-page section snapping. Container: overflow-x:auto; scroll-snap-type: x mandatory; flex row. Each slide: flex:0 0 <width>; scroll-snap-align: start (or center). Use 'proximity' instead of 'mandatory' if mandatory feels too aggressive. Add prev/next buttons calling scrollBy for a fuller carousel; native scroll already gives touch/keyboard support.

effects.title: Scroll Velocity Skew
effects.summary: Content skews and squashes in proportion to scroll speed, then springs back to rest — a fluid, physical scroll feel.
effects.description: Tracks scroll velocity each frame and maps it to a skewY (and slight scale) on the content, which eases back to zero when you stop. Fast flicks bend the page; slow scrolls barely move. A signature 'smooth scroll' flourish on award-style sites.
effects.performance_notes: A single requestAnimationFrame loop computing velocity and writing one transform. transform-only — GPU-friendly. Loop is cheap and self-limiting.
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative motion; content remains fully readable and in order. Disabled entirely under prefers-reduced-motion. Clamp max skew so text never becomes unreadable.
effects.ai_usage: Use for premium/creative sites to add tactile momentum. Each frame, compute velocity = scrollY − lastScrollY, smooth it toward 0 with a decay (lerp), clamp to ±--max-skew, and write transform: skewY(v) (optionally scaleY). Apply to a wrapper, not body. Clamp so text stays legible and disable under prefers-reduced-motion. Use one rAF loop, not a scroll listener per event.
effects.source:

effects.title: Sticky Stacking Cards
effects.summary: Cards stick to the top and stack on one another as you scroll past them.
effects.description: Each card uses position: sticky with the same top offset so successive cards pin and overlap, building a layered stack — a trendy storytelling/section pattern requiring no JS.
effects.performance_notes: Pure layout/paint; very efficient, no scroll JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Content remains in natural reading order; safe under reduced-motion (no autonomous animation).
variations: Scale/rotate each card slightly as it stacks (scroll-driven)
Offset top per card for a fanned stack
effects.ai_usage: Use for feature walkthroughs and editorial sections. Give each card position:sticky; top:<offset>; the cards naturally pin and overlap as you scroll. Add a slight scale-down via scroll-driven animation for the 'deck' feel. Ensure enough scroll distance between cards. The demo auto-scrolls to show the stacking in the catalog frame.

effects.title: SVG Animated Connector
effects.summary: A curved dashed line that draws itself between two nodes and then flows, like a live data/flow diagram link.
effects.description: An SVG path connecting two boxes draws in via stroke-dashoffset, then a marching-ants dash flow animates continuously to imply data moving along it. The look of flow builders, integrations diagrams, and 'how it works' graphics.
effects.performance_notes: One draw-in transition then a looping dashoffset animation on a single path. Cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative diagram; convey the relationship in adjacent text too. The flow loop stops under prefers-reduced-motion (show the drawn line).
variations: Add an arrowhead marker at the end
Animate a dot traveling the path (offset-path)
effects.ai_usage: Use for 'how it works', integration maps, and flow/pipeline graphics. Draw a path between two nodes; animate stroke-dashoffset from pathLength to 0 to 'draw' it, then switch to a looping dash flow (animate stroke-dashoffset continuously with a fixed stroke-dasharray) for 'marching ants'. Add an arrowhead marker if directional, describe the relationship in text, and stop the loop under reduced motion.

effects.title: SVG Animated Gradient Stroke
effects.summary: A circular progress/loader ring stroked with a gradient and an animating dash.
effects.description: Combines an SVG linearGradient stroke with stroke-linecap round and an animated stroke-dasharray/dashoffset to create a smooth gradient-stroked spinner or progress arc.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. Note: SVG gradient stroke doesn't rotate with the element, so rotate the whole SVG, not the gradient.
effects.performance_notes: Animating rotation + dash via CSS is cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: If used as a loader, wrap with role=status + a visually-hidden label. Slow under prefers-reduced-motion rather than stopping (it's a busy indicator).
variations: Determinate progress (set dashoffset from a value)
effects.ai_usage: Use for premium loaders and progress rings. Stroke a <circle> with url(#gradient); set stroke-linecap='round'. For an indeterminate spinner, animate the whole SVG's rotation AND the stroke-dasharray/dashoffset (Material-style). For determinate progress, fix dasharray to the circumference and set dashoffset from your percentage. Rotate the SVG element, not the gradient (gradients are in user space). Add role=status + label when it's a loader.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: SVG Animated Logo Reveal
effects.summary: A logo mark that draws its outline (stroke), then fills with color — the classic brand intro / splash reveal.
effects.description: Combines two SVG techniques into a brand moment: first the logo's paths stroke themselves on via stroke-dashoffset, then the fill fades in and the wordmark slides up. Ideal as a page/app splash or header entrance.
effects.performance_notes: stroke-dashoffset draw + an opacity fill fade + a small translate on the wordmark. One-shot, then static.
effects.accessibility_notes: Give the SVG role='img' and a <title> (the brand name) so it has an accessible name. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show the final filled logo immediately.
variations: Stroke stays as an outline logo
effects.ai_usage: Use as a splash/intro or header logo entrance. Set each path's stroke-dasharray to its length, offset it hidden, animate offset to 0 to 'draw', then transition fill-opacity from 0 to 1 (delayed) and slide the wordmark up. Add role='img' + <title>=brand name for a11y and show the final filled state instantly under reduced motion.
effects.source: VERTEX

effects.title: SVG Animated Wave Divider
effects.summary: Layered SVG waves that drift at different speeds to separate two sections with gentle, looping motion.
effects.description: Three stacked wave paths translate horizontally at different rates, creating parallax water-like motion between page sections. A modern replacement for a flat section border, popular on landing pages.
effects.performance_notes: Three CSS translateX loops on wide wave paths. transform-only; cheap. Pauses under reduced-motion.
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative (aria-hidden). Ensure adequate contrast between the two sections it divides.
variations: Single wave for a calmer look
effects.ai_usage: Use between landing-page sections for organic separation. Stack 2–3 wide wave <path>s (≥200% width) and animate each translateX from 0 to -50% at different durations for parallax. Match each wave's fill to the adjacent section color. Mark the SVG aria-hidden and pause animation under prefers-reduced-motion.

effects.title: SVG Annotation Arrow
effects.summary: A hand-drawn marker arrow that draws itself in to point at something — the 'look here' annotation seen on landing pages.
effects.description: A curved, slightly wobbly SVG arrow with a drawn-on arrowhead that animates via stroke-dashoffset to point from a label to a target (a button, a feature). Adds a human, marker-pen 'notice this' cue.
effects.performance_notes: Two stroke-dashoffset animations (shaft + head). Trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative (aria-hidden); never rely on it alone — keep the label text it points from. Draws instantly under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Loop the draw to keep nudging
effects.ai_usage: Use to point at a CTA, a new feature, or a form field with a friendly hand-drawn cue. Draw a curved shaft path + a 2-stroke arrowhead; animate each one's stroke-dashoffset from full to 0 (head after the shaft). Keep it aria-hidden and pair it with the real label text; draw instantly under reduced motion. Position relative to the target you're annotating.
effects.source: it's free!

effects.title: SVG Gooey Filter
effects.summary: Blobs that merge with a liquid 'metaball' connection using an SVG blur + contrast filter.
effects.description: The classic gooey effect: feGaussianBlur softens shapes, then feColorMatrix cranks alpha contrast so overlapping blurred edges snap together into a liquid blob. Applied to CSS-animated circles.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. The filter can be defined in a 0-size inline SVG and referenced from CSS via filter: url(#goo).
effects.performance_notes: SVG blur filters are moderately expensive; keep the filtered area small and the element count low. Motion is CSS transform (cheap).
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative; blob motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion. The contrast matrix can produce hard edges — keep it off text.

customization.name: feColorMatrix alpha row
customization.description: The '18 -7' values tune the snap threshold

variations: Gooey menu (FAB that spits out items)
Colored goo with a gradient fill
effects.ai_usage: Use for playful liquid UI: gooey menus, merging loader dots, blob backgrounds. Define filter#goo = feGaussianBlur(stdDeviation ~10) → feColorMatrix(... 18 -7) → feComposite. Apply filter:url(#goo) to a CONTAINER whose children (solid circles) overlap; the blur+contrast makes them fuse. Tune stdDeviation for goo length and the matrix's last two alpha numbers for the merge threshold. Keep it off text and gate motion under reduced-motion.

effects.title: SVG Grain / Noise Texture
effects.summary: A subtle film-grain overlay generated entirely by feTurbulence — no image asset.
effects.description: feTurbulence produces fractal noise rendered to a rect, used as a low-opacity overlay to add tactile grain over flat colors and gradients (the trendy 'noise' texture), with zero network cost.
effects.performance_notes: Generating noise once is cheap; do NOT animate it (re-rendering noise every frame is expensive). Reuse one noise layer as a fixed overlay.
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative; keep opacity low so it never reduces text contrast. No motion.
variations: Heavier grain for a poster look
Scope the overlay to one card
effects.ai_usage: Use to add tactile grain over flat fills, gradients, and mesh backgrounds (kills banding, adds a premium analog feel). Generate noise with feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency ~0.8, numOctaves~3); render it to a full-size rect; place that as a position:fixed/absolute overlay at low opacity (0.04-0.08), pointer-events:none, above the background but below text. Do NOT animate the turbulence (costly) — a static layer is enough. Optionally desaturate it with feColorMatrix.
effects.source: Grain over gradient

effects.title: SVG Like Burst
effects.summary: A heart that pops and emits a radial particle burst when liked — the satisfying 'favorite' micro-interaction.
effects.description: Clicking the heart toggles its filled state with a spring 'pop' and fires a one-shot burst of dots/sparks radiating outward (Twitter/Instagram-style). A high-reward micro-interaction for likes, saves, and favorites.
effects.performance_notes: The burst is a set of pre-placed particles animated once via a toggled class; the heart scales with a keyframe. No per-frame JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: It's a real <button> with aria-pressed reflecting liked state and an accessible label that updates (Like/Unlike). Burst is decorative (aria-hidden) and skipped under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Star or bookmark instead of heart
effects.ai_usage: Use for like/save/favorite buttons. Make it a <button> with aria-pressed; on activate, toggle the filled heart with a spring pop and play a one-shot particle burst (radial dots animated by a toggled class, removed after). Reflect state in aria-pressed + label, keep the burst aria-hidden, and skip the burst/pop under prefers-reduced-motion.

effects.title: SVG Line Draw
effects.summary: A path that draws itself on, like a pen tracing a signature or logo.
effects.description: Sets stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset to the path length, then animates the offset to 0 with CSS so the stroke appears to be drawn. The canonical SVG entrance for logos, signatures, and line icons.
effects.performance_notes: Animating stroke-dashoffset is GPU-friendly. Set the dasharray/offset to the path's total length (pathLength attribute lets you use a round number like 100).
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; give the SVG role=img + a title/aria-label if it conveys meaning. Animation is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion (path shown complete).

customization.name: pathLength
customization.description: Normalizes length so dasharray can be 100

variations: Stagger multiple paths to draw in sequence
Fill the shape in after the stroke completes
effects.ai_usage: Use for logo/signature reveals and line-icon entrances. Add pathLength='100' to each path so you can set stroke-dasharray:100 and animate stroke-dashoffset from 100 to 0 in CSS. Multiple paths: give each an animation-delay to draw in order. To 'ink in' a filled logo, fade fill from transparent to solid after the stroke finishes. Gate with prefers-reduced-motion (show offset:0).
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Line Draw</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-line-draw",
    "title": "SVG Line Draw",
    "summary": "A path that draws itself on, like a pen tracing a signature or logo.",
    "description": "Sets stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset to the path length, then animates the offset to 0 with CSS so the stroke appears to be drawn. The canonical SVG entrance for logos, signatures, and line icons.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "animation", "stroke"],
    "tags": ["svg", "line-draw", "stroke-dashoffset", "signature", "logo", "draw-on"],
    "tech": ["svg", "css"],
    "era": "2010s",
    "difficulty": "intermediate",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers.",
    "performance_notes": "Animating stroke-dashoffset is GPU-friendly. Set the dasharray/offset to the path's total length (pathLength attribute lets you use a round number like 100).",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative; give the SVG role=img + a title/aria-label if it conveys meaning. Animation is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion (path shown complete).",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "pathLength", "description": "Normalizes length so dasharray can be 100", "default": "100" },
      { "name": "--dur", "description": "Draw duration", "default": "2.5s" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Stagger multiple paths to draw in sequence", "Fill the shape in after the stroke completes", "Loop by alternating the offset"],
    "related": ["svg-animated-checkmark", "typewriter-text"],
    "ai_usage": "Use for logo/signature reveals and line-icon entrances. Add pathLength='100' to each path so you can set stroke-dasharray:100 and animate stroke-dashoffset from 100 to 0 in CSS. Multiple paths: give each an animation-delay to draw in order. To 'ink in' a filled logo, fade fill from transparent to solid after the stroke finishes. Gate with prefers-reduced-motion (show offset:0)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    :root { --dur: 2.5s; }
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #0b0d12; }
    .draw path { stroke: #7c5cff; stroke-width: 3; fill: none; stroke-linecap: round; stroke-linejoin: round;
      stroke-dasharray: 100; stroke-dashoffset: 100; animation: draw var(--dur) ease forwards infinite; }
    .draw path:nth-child(2) { stroke: #2dd4bf; animation-delay: .4s; }
    .draw path:nth-child(3) { stroke: #f0abfc; animation-delay: .8s; }
    @keyframes draw { 0% { stroke-dashoffset: 100; } 70%, 100% { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
    @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .draw path { animation: none; stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg class="draw" width="220" height="160" viewBox="0 0 220 160" role="img" aria-label="Drawing line art">
    <path pathLength="100" d="M20,120 C40,40 80,40 100,120 S160,40 200,120" />
    <path pathLength="100" d="M20,80 H200" />
    <path pathLength="100" d="M110,20 V140" />
  </svg>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: SVG Map Route Draw
effects.summary: A dashed travel route draws itself across a stylized map between two pins, then a marker glides along the path.
effects.description: Combines a self-drawing path (stroke-dashoffset) with a marker that travels the same path via SMIL animateMotion — the 'journey' / delivery-tracking / trip-planner graphic. Start and end pins drop in, the route draws, then the dot rides it.
effects.performance_notes: One dashoffset draw + one animateMotion along an mpath. Lightweight; loops.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; describe the journey ('A → B') in adjacent text. The marker motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion (show the drawn route).
effects.ai_usage: Use for trip planners, delivery tracking, and 'how it works' journeys. Draw the route path with stroke-dashoffset, then move a marker along the SAME path using <animateMotion><mpath href='#route'/>. Drop start/end pins, describe the route in text for a11y, and pause the marker (show the finished route) under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.source: ◍ Old TownHarbor Pier ◍

effects.title: SVG Morphing Blob Loader
effects.summary: A gradient blob that endlessly morphs between organic shapes while slowly rotating — a friendlier alternative to a spinner.
effects.description: An SVG path whose 'd' attribute animates through several blob shapes (SMIL), filled with an animated gradient and gently rotating. A warmer, on-brand loading indicator for playful or premium products.
effects.browser_support: SMIL path morphing works in all current browsers; rotation/gradient via CSS.
effects.performance_notes: One SMIL d-morph + a CSS rotation. Keep the loop under ~3s so it feels intentional.
effects.accessibility_notes: Add role='status' and a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label so the state is announced. Under prefers-reduced-motion, stop the morph (show one shape).
variations: Use as an idle mascot, not just loading
effects.ai_usage: Use as a branded loading indicator. Animate an SVG path's d through a few blob shapes that share point count (SMIL <animate attributeName='d'> with values, calcMode='spline'), fill with a gradient, and add a slow CSS rotation. Wrap with role='status' + a visually-hidden 'Loading…' label, keep the loop short, and freeze to one shape under reduced motion.

effects.title: SVG Path Morph
effects.summary: One shape smoothly morphs into another by animating the path's d attribute (SMIL).
effects.description: Uses SMIL <animate attributeName='d'> to tween a path between several shapes. Morphing requires each shape's path to have the SAME command structure / point count.
effects.browser_support: SMIL animate of `d` works in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari. (CSS `d` interpolation is Chromium-only; SMIL is the most portable no-JS option.)
effects.performance_notes: Cheap for a single path. The hard part is authoring compatible paths, not runtime cost.
effects.accessibility_notes: SMIL does NOT honor prefers-reduced-motion automatically — the included script pauses SVG animations when reduced motion is requested. Decorative; add a title if meaningful.

customization.name: values
customization.description: Semicolon-separated d strings to morph between

variations: Morph icons (play↔pause) on state change via JS
Use a JS lib (flubber) when point counts can't match
effects.ai_usage: Use to tween between shapes/icons. CRITICAL: every shape in the `values` list must share the same path command sequence and point count, or the morph jumps. Author them by editing one base path's coordinates. SMIL <animate attributeName='d' values='A;B;C;A' dur=... repeatCount='indefinite'> is the most cross-browser no-JS route. For arbitrary shapes with mismatched points, use a JS morphing lib (flubber/GSAP). SMIL ignores reduced-motion, so pause via svg.pauseAnimations() (shown).

<head>

  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {

    "performance_notes": "Cheap for a single path. The hard part is authoring compatible paths, not runtime cost.",
    "accessibility_notes": "SMIL does NOT honor prefers-reduced-motion automatically — the included script pauses SVG animations when reduced motion is requested. Decorative; add a title if meaningful.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "values", "description": "Semicolon-separated d strings to morph between", "default": "see source" },
      { "name": "dur", "description": "Full cycle duration", "default": "8s" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Morph icons (play↔pause) on state change via JS", "Blob background morph", "Use a JS lib (flubber) when point counts can't match"],
    "related": ["blob-morph", "svg-line-draw"],
    "ai_usage": "Use to tween between shapes/icons. CRITICAL: every shape in the `values` list must share the same path command sequence and point count, or the morph jumps. Author them by editing one base path's coordinates. SMIL <animate attributeName='d' values='A;B;C;A' dur=... repeatCount='indefinite'> is the most cross-browser no-JS route. For arbitrary shapes with mismatched points, use a JS morphing lib (flubber/GSAP). SMIL ignores reduced-motion, so pause via svg.pauseAnimations() (shown)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #0b0d12; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg width="220" height="220" viewBox="0 0 200 200" role="img" aria-label="Morphing shape">
    <defs>
      <linearGradient id="mg" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1">
        <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#7c5cff" /><stop offset="100%" stop-color="#2dd4bf" />
      </linearGradient>
    </defs>
    <path fill="url(#mg)" d="M100,30 C140,30 170,60 170,100 C170,140 140,170 100,170 C60,170 30,140 30,100 C30,60 60,30 100,30 Z">
      <animate attributeName="d" dur="8s" repeatCount="indefinite"
        values="
          M100,30 C140,30 170,60 170,100 C170,140 140,170 100,170 C60,170 30,140 30,100 C30,60 60,30 100,30 Z;
          M100,20 C150,40 180,70 165,115 C150,160 120,180 95,170 C55,155 25,135 30,90 C35,50 60,30 100,20 Z;
          M100,35 C135,25 175,55 165,100 C175,145 135,175 100,165 C65,175 25,145 35,100 C25,55 65,45 100,35 Z;
          M100,30 C140,30 170,60 170,100 C170,140 140,170 100,170 C60,170 30,140 30,100 C30,60 60,30 100,30 Z" />
    </path>
  </svg>
  <script>
    if (matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {
      document.querySelectorAll("svg").forEach(function (s) { if (s.pauseAnimations) s.pauseAnimations(); });

effects.title: SVG Progress Ring
effects.summary: A circular progress meter: a gradient ring sweeps to a target percentage while the number counts up to match.
effects.description: An SVG circle uses stroke-dasharray/offset to fill to a set percentage along a track, paired with a JS count-up of the value. The canonical dashboard/stat ring (storage used, score, completion).
effects.performance_notes: One stroke-dashoffset transition + a short rAF count-up. Trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Expose value via role='progressbar' with aria-valuenow/min/max so it's not visual-only. Under reduced-motion, set the final value instantly (no sweep/count).
effects.ai_usage: Use for dashboards, scores, quotas, and completion meters. Draw a track circle and a value circle; set the value circle's stroke-dasharray to its circumference and animate stroke-dashoffset to circumference×(1−p/100); count the label up in parallel. Add role='progressbar' + aria-valuenow/min/max, and set final state instantly under reduced motion.
effects.source: 0%

effects.title: SVG Squiggle Underline
effects.summary: A hand-drawn wavy underline that draws itself on hover/focus — a playful, human alternative to a flat underline.
effects.description: An inline SVG squiggle path sits under a link; on hover or focus its stroke draws in via stroke-dashoffset, giving a marker-pen, hand-made feel. Common on editorial, portfolio, and friendly brand sites.
effects.performance_notes: One stroke-dashoffset transition per link. Trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Underline also appears on :focus-visible (not hover only) so keyboard users get the cue; the link remains a real <a>. Decorative SVG is aria-hidden.
effects.ai_usage: Use for links, nav items, and emphasized words on editorial/portfolio/brand sites. Place an absolutely-positioned inline SVG squiggle under the text; set its path stroke-dasharray to the path length and offset it fully (hidden), then animate offset to 0 on :hover AND :focus-visible. Mark the SVG aria-hidden and keep the <a> real.
effects.source: We design things worth a second look.

effects.title: SVG Stroke-to-Fill Text
effects.summary: Big SVG text writes itself as an outline (stroke), then floods with a gradient fill — a premium headline/intro reveal.
effects.description: SVG <text> with a gradient stroke animates its stroke-dashoffset so the letters appear hand-written, then a gradient fill fades in. The outline-then-fill technique scaled to a wordmark or hero headline.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (SVG text stroke + dasharray).
effects.performance_notes: stroke-dashoffset draw + a fill-opacity fade. One-shot then static.
effects.accessibility_notes: Give the <svg> role='img' and an <title> with the words so it has an accessible name. Show the filled text immediately under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero wordmarks and intros. Render SVG <text> with fill:none and a gradient stroke; set stroke-dasharray large and animate stroke-dashoffset to 0 to 'write' it, then transition fill-opacity 0→1 (delayed) for the flood. Add role='img' + <title> for a11y, tune dasharray to the glyph perimeter, and show final state instantly under reduced motion.

effects.title: SVG Success Checkmark
effects.summary: A success animation: the circle draws around, then the tick strokes in, with a pop.
effects.description: Two stroked SVG paths (circle + check) animate their stroke-dashoffset in sequence, plus a subtle scale pop — the satisfying confirmation seen after form submits and payments.
effects.performance_notes: Two stroke-dashoffset animations + one transform; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Pair with a text message and aria-live so success isn't conveyed by the icon alone. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show the completed check instantly.
variations: Replace a loading spinner on completion
effects.ai_usage: Use to confirm completed async actions (submit, pay, save), ideally swapping in where a spinner was. Stroke a circle path and a check path; animate each one's stroke-dashoffset from full to 0, the tick delayed until the circle finishes; add a small scale pop. ALWAYS accompany with a text status + aria-live region — color/icon alone isn't accessible. Show the final state instantly under reduced motion. Demo loops for visibility.
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Success Checkmark</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-animated-checkmark",
    "title": "SVG Success Checkmark",
    "summary": "A success animation: the circle draws around, then the tick strokes in, with a pop.",
    "description": "Two stroked SVG paths (circle + check) animate their stroke-dashoffset in sequence, plus a subtle scale pop — the satisfying confirmation seen after form submits and payments.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "animation", "feedback"],
    "tags": ["svg", "checkmark", "success", "confirmation", "stroke", "tick"],
    "tech": ["svg", "css"],
    "era": "2020s",
    "difficulty": "intermediate",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers.",
    "performance_notes": "Two stroke-dashoffset animations + one transform; trivial.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Pair with a text message and aria-live so success isn't conveyed by the icon alone. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show the completed check instantly.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "--ok", "description": "Success color", "default": "#22c55e" },
      { "name": "timing", "description": "Circle then tick durations/delays", "default": "0.5s + 0.3s" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Error variant (red circle + X)", "Replace a loading spinner on completion", "Bounce the whole badge in"],
    "related": ["svg-line-draw", "animated-checkbox", "loading-button"],
    "ai_usage": "Use to confirm completed async actions (submit, pay, save), ideally swapping in where a spinner was. Stroke a circle path and a check path; animate each one's stroke-dashoffset from full to 0, the tick delayed until the circle finishes; add a small scale pop. ALWAYS accompany with a text status + aria-live region — color/icon alone isn't accessible. Show the final state instantly under reduced motion. Demo loops for visibility."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    :root { --ok: #22c55e; }
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #0b0d12; }
    .check { animation: pop .4s ease both; }
    .circle { stroke: var(--ok); stroke-width: 5; fill: none; stroke-dasharray: 166; stroke-dashoffset: 166;
      animation: stroke .5s cubic-bezier(.65,0,.45,1) forwards; }
    .tick { stroke: var(--ok); stroke-width: 6; fill: none; stroke-linecap: round; stroke-linejoin: round;
      stroke-dasharray: 48; stroke-dashoffset: 48; animation: stroke .3s cubic-bezier(.65,0,.45,1) .5s forwards; }
    @keyframes stroke { to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
    @keyframes pop { 0% { transform: scale(.7); } 60% { transform: scale(1.05); } 100% { transform: scale(1); } }
    @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
      .circle, .tick { animation: none; stroke-dashoffset: 0; } .check { animation: none; }
    }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg class="check" width="120" height="120" viewBox="0 0 60 60" role="img" aria-label="Success">
    <circle class="circle" cx="30" cy="30" r="26" />
    <path class="tick" d="M18,31 l8,8 l16,-17" />
  </svg>
  <script>
    // Replay on a loop so the draw-in is visible in the catalog frame.
    if (!matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {
      var svg = document.querySelector(".check");
      setInterval(function () {
        svg.querySelectorAll(".circle,.tick").forEach(function (p) { p.style.animation = "none"; });
        svg.style.animation = "none"; void svg.offsetWidth;
        svg.querySelectorAll(".circle,.tick").forEach(function (p) { p.style.animation = ""; });
        svg.style.animation = "";
      }, 2600);
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: SVG Sun / Moon Toggle
effects.summary: A dark-mode switch where the sun morphs into a crescent moon — rays retract, a mask carves the crescent, the whole mark rotates.
effects.description: The beloved theme-toggle micro-interaction built in SVG: clicking morphs a rayed sun into a crescent moon (and flips the demo's theme). The crescent is made by a moving mask circle; rays scale/rotate out. Smooth, reversible, satisfying.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (SVG mask, CSS transitions).
effects.performance_notes: Transitions on transform/r/cx and ray scale — no per-frame JS. The mask carves the crescent without extra DOM.
effects.accessibility_notes: It's a real <button> with role inferred, aria-pressed reflecting dark state, and a label that updates (Switch to dark/light). Honors prefers-reduced-motion (instant swap) and persists nothing here (wire to your theme).
effects.ai_usage: Use as a dark-mode toggle. Build a sun = central circle + rays; to make the moon, transition a mask circle over the main circle to carve a crescent while scaling rays to 0 and rotating the group. Make it a real <button> with aria-pressed + an updating label, wire the click to your theme switch (e.g. data-theme + persisted preference), and swap instantly under prefers-reduced-motion.

  </script>
  <style>
    :root{--sun:#f7b733;--moon:#cdd5e0;}
    html,body{height:100%;margin:0;transition:background .4s,color .4s}
    body{display:grid;place-items:center;background:#0b0d12;color:#e8ecf6;font-family:system-ui,sans-serif}
    body.light{background:#f3f4f6;color:#1a1c22}
    .toggle{background:none;border:1px solid currentColor;border-radius:999px;padding:.7rem;cursor:pointer;color:inherit;display:grid;place-items:center;
      width:64px;height:64px;opacity:.85;transition:opacity .2s}
    .toggle:hover{opacity:1}
    .toggle:focus-visible{outline:3px solid #7c5cff;outline-offset:3px}
    .icon{width:30px;height:30px;overflow:visible}
    .core{fill:var(--moon);transition:fill .4s}
    body.light .core{fill:var(--sun)}
    .icon{transform:rotate(40deg);transition:transform .5s cubic-bezier(.5,0,.2,1)}
    body.light .icon{transform:rotate(0deg)}
    .rays line{stroke:var(--sun);stroke-width:2.5;stroke-linecap:round;transform-origin:center;transform:scale(0);opacity:0;transition:transform .4s,opacity .3s}
    body.light .rays line{transform:scale(1);opacity:1}
    .mask-c{transition:cx .5s cubic-bezier(.5,0,.2,1),cy .5s}
    @media(prefers-reduced-motion:reduce){html,body,.icon,.rays line,.core,.mask-c{transition:none}}
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <button class="toggle" id="t" aria-pressed="true" aria-label="Switch to light mode">
    <svg class="icon" viewBox="0 0 24 24" aria-hidden="true">
      <defs><mask id="moon"><rect width="24" height="24" fill="#fff"/><circle class="mask-c" id="mc" cx="24" cy="2" r="8" fill="#000"/></mask></defs>
      <g class="rays"><line x1="12" y1="1" x2="12" y2="3"/><line x1="12" y1="21" x2="12" y2="23"/><line x1="1" y1="12" x2="3" y2="12"/><line x1="21" y1="12" x2="23" y2="12"/><line x1="4" y1="4" x2="5.5" y2="5.5"/><line x1="18.5" y1="18.5" x2="20" y2="20"/><line x1="4" y1="20" x2="5.5" y2="18.5"/><line x1="18.5" y1="5.5" x2="20" y2="4"/></g>
      <circle class="core" cx="12" cy="12" r="6" mask="url(#moon)"/>
    </svg>
  </button>
  <script>
    var t=document.getElementById('t'),mc=document.getElementById('mc');
    function apply(dark){
      document.body.classList.toggle('light',!dark);
      t.setAttribute('aria-pressed',dark);
      t.setAttribute('aria-label',dark?'Switch to light mode':'Switch to dark mode');
      // move mask circle: dark = crescent (mask over), light = sun (mask off-screen)
      mc.setAttribute('cx',dark?'17':'24');mc.setAttribute('cy',dark?'7':'2');
    }
    apply(true);
    t.addEventListener('click',function(){apply(t.getAttribute('aria-pressed')!=='true');});
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: SVG Text on a Path
effects.summary: Text that flows along a curve, optionally scrolling around it — circular badges and arched headings.
effects.description: Uses <textPath href='#path'> to lay text along an arbitrary path, with an animated startOffset to make it travel — perfect for circular seal/badge text and curved labels.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (textPath). startOffset animation via SMIL works broadly.
effects.performance_notes: Cheap. Define the path once and reference it; hide the path stroke if you only want the text.
effects.accessibility_notes: SVG text is real, selectable, and readable by screen readers — better than image text. Add role=img + aria-label on the SVG if the curved text is part of a logo. SMIL motion ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses it.

customization.name: path d
customization.description: The curve the text follows (arc, circle, wave)

customization.name: startOffset
customization.description: Where text begins along the path (animate to scroll)

variations: Text along a wavy or spiral path
effects.ai_usage: Use for circular badges/seals, arched titles, and curved labels. Define a <path id='p'> (a circle drawn as a path for a ring of text, or an arc for an arch) and put <text><textPath href='#p'>…</textPath></text>. Set the path's stroke to none if you only want text. To rotate the text around the ring, animate startOffset 0%→100% (SMIL) — pause under reduced motion. SVG text stays accessible/selectable.
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Text on a Path</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-text-on-path",
    "title": "SVG Text on a Path",
    "summary": "Text that flows along a curve, optionally scrolling around it — circular badges and arched headings.",
    "description": "Uses <textPath href='#path'> to lay text along an arbitrary path, with an animated startOffset to make it travel — perfect for circular seal/badge text and curved labels.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",

    "accessibility_notes": "SVG text is real, selectable, and readable by screen readers — better than image text. Add role=img + aria-label on the SVG if the curved text is part of a logo. SMIL motion ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses it.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "path d", "description": "The curve the text follows (arc, circle, wave)", "default": "circle path" },

Define a <path id='p'> (a circle drawn as a path for a ring of text, or an arc for an arch) and put <text><textPath href='#p'>…</textPath></text>. Set the path's stroke to none if you only want text. To rotate the text around the ring, animate startOffset 0%→100% (SMIL) — pause under reduced motion. SVG text stays accessible/selectable."
  }

  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
