effects.title: SVG Turbulence Distortion
effects.summary: A wavy, liquid distortion driven by feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap, animated.
effects.description: feTurbulence generates Perlin-style noise that feDisplacementMap uses to push pixels around, creating a rippling/heat-haze/liquid warp. An animated baseFrequency makes the distortion flow.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers support the filter primitives. Animating baseFrequency via SMIL works broadly.
effects.performance_notes: Displacement filters are GPU-heavy at large sizes — keep the filtered region modest and the scale low. Static distortion is much cheaper than animated.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative. SMIL ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses animation when requested. Avoid distorting readable text.
variations: Heat-haze on a hero image (low scale)
Hover-only distortion via JS toggling the filter
effects.ai_usage: Use for liquid/wavy/heat-haze treatments on shapes, images, or logos. Pipeline: feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency=low) → feDisplacementMap(in='SourceGraphic', scale=N). Increase scale for stronger warp; animate baseFrequency (SMIL) to make it flow. Keep scale modest on text or it becomes unreadable, and keep the area small for performance. Pause via svg.pauseAnimations() under reduced motion (shown).
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Turbulence Distortion</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-turbulence-distortion",
    "title": "SVG Turbulence Distortion",
    "summary": "A wavy, liquid distortion driven by feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap, animated.",
    "description": "feTurbulence generates Perlin-style noise that feDisplacementMap uses to push pixels around, creating a rippling/heat-haze/liquid warp. An animated baseFrequency makes the distortion flow.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "filter", "distortion"],
    "tags": ["svg", "feturbulence", "displacement", "distortion", "ripple", "liquid", "noise"],
    "tech": ["svg"],
    "era": "2020s",
    "difficulty": "advanced",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers support the filter primitives. Animating baseFrequency via SMIL works broadly.",
    "performance_notes": "Displacement filters are GPU-heavy at large sizes — keep the filtered region modest and the scale low. Static distortion is much cheaper than animated.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative. SMIL ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses animation when requested. Avoid distorting readable text.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "feDisplacementMap scale", "description": "Distortion strength", "default": "18" },
      { "name": "baseFrequency", "description": "Noise scale (smaller = larger waves)", "default": "0.012" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Heat-haze on a hero image (low scale)", "Flag wave on text/logo", "Hover-only distortion via JS toggling the filter"],
    "related": ["svg-noise-texture", "blob-morph"],
    "ai_usage": "Use for liquid/wavy/heat-haze treatments on shapes, images, or logos. Pipeline: feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency=low) → feDisplacementMap(in='SourceGraphic', scale=N). Increase scale for stronger warp; animate baseFrequency (SMIL) to make it flow. Keep scale modest on text or it becomes unreadable, and keep the area small for performance. Pause via svg.pauseAnimations() under reduced motion (shown)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #06060c; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg width="300" height="200" viewBox="0 0 300 200" role="img" aria-label="Distorted shape">
    <defs>
      <filter id="warp">
        <feTurbulence type="fractalNoise" baseFrequency="0.012 0.02" numOctaves="2" seed="7" result="noise">
          <animate attributeName="baseFrequency" dur="14s" repeatCount="indefinite"
            values="0.012 0.02; 0.02 0.012; 0.012 0.02" />
        </feTurbulence>
        <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="noise" scale="18" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
      </filter>
      <linearGradient id="wg" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1">
        <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#7c5cff" /><stop offset="100%" stop-color="#2dd4bf" />
      </linearGradient>
    </defs>
    <g filter="url(#warp)">
      <rect x="40" y="40" width="220" height="120" rx="20" fill="url(#wg)" />
      <text x="150" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="40" font-weight="800" fill="#fff" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">LIQUID</text>
    </g>
  </svg>
  <script>
    if (matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {
      document.querySelectorAll("svg").forEach(function (s) { if (s.pauseAnimations) s.pauseAnimations(); });
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: Animated Gradient Text
effects.summary: Letters filled with a moving multi-stop gradient via background-clip: text.
effects.description: Paints a wide linear-gradient as the element background, clips it to the glyph shapes, and animates background-position to make the colors flow through the text.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. Needs -webkit-background-clip: text (still required even in Chromium).
effects.performance_notes: Animating background-position is GPU-friendly and cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: The text remains real, selectable text. Animation pauses under prefers-reduced-motion. Verify the lightest gradient stop has enough contrast.
variations: Conic gradient for a rainbow sweep
Pair with a transparent text-stroke outline
effects.ai_usage: Ideal for headings, brand names, and marketing hero text. Apply .grad-text to an inline-block heading. Set --grad to your palette and keep the gradient at least 2x wider than the text (background-size:200%) so the animation has room to travel. Works on any background. Remove the animation property for a static gradient fill.
effects.source: Gradient

effects.title: Glitch Text
effects.summary: RGB-split datamosh glitch using duplicated pseudo-elements and clip-path jitter.
effects.description: Duplicates the text via ::before and ::after offset in cyan and magenta, then animates clip-path bands to create a chromatic-aberration / signal-glitch effect.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (clip-path inset is widely supported).
effects.performance_notes: Two extra painted layers plus clip-path animation; fine for a few headings, avoid on long body text.
effects.accessibility_notes: Can be distracting/seizure-adjacent; the motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion, leaving a static RGB-split. The text is duplicated via data-text and aria stays on the real element.
variations: Increase clip band frequency for heavier corruption
Add skew transforms for VHS feel
effects.ai_usage: Use for cyberpunk/gaming/tech headlines. Requires the text to be duplicated in a data-text attribute that matches the visible text exactly (the pseudo-elements read content: attr(data-text)). Apply .glitch to a heading. Best on dark backgrounds. Consider gating the animation behind :hover for calmer pages.
effects.source: GLITCH

effects.title: Highlighter Sweep
effects.summary: A marker-pen highlight sweeps in behind key words when they scroll into view — drawing the eye to the phrase that matters.
effects.description: A skewed highlight band animates its width from 0 to full behind emphasized words as they enter the viewport (or on hover), mimicking a highlighter stroke. A favorite editorial / landing emphasis device.
effects.performance_notes: A background-size transition triggered once by IntersectionObserver. transform/size only.
effects.accessibility_notes: The word stays normal readable text; the highlight is a decorative background. Reduced-motion shows the highlight already filled (no sweep).
variations: Sweep on hover instead of scroll
effects.ai_usage: Use to emphasize a key phrase in a headline or paragraph. Wrap the word in a span with a linear-gradient background sized 0% width; when it scrolls into view (IntersectionObserver) transition background-size to 100%. Keep the text color readable over the marker, keep it real text, and show it pre-filled under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.source: We don't just build websites — we build unfair advantages.

effects.title: Neon Glow Text
effects.summary: Glowing neon-sign lettering built from layered text-shadows with a soft flicker.
effects.description: Stacks several increasingly-blurred text-shadows in the brand color to fake the bloom of a gas neon tube, plus an optional flicker animation.
effects.browser_support: All browsers; text-shadow is universally supported.
effects.performance_notes: Multiple text-shadows are cheap. The flicker animates opacity/text-shadow on a single element — negligible cost.
effects.accessibility_notes: Ensure sufficient contrast against the dark background. The flicker is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Swap --neon per word for multi-color signage
Remove the flicker for a steady tube
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero headings, logos, or playful CTAs with a retro/cyberpunk vibe on dark backgrounds. Apply the .neon class to any inline text element. Tune --neon for the tube color and keep the background dark for the glow to read. The glow is purely text-shadow, so it scales with font-size automatically. Always keep the prefers-reduced-motion guard if you keep the flicker.
effects.source: NEON

effects.title: Outline / Stroke Text
effects.summary: Hollow outlined lettering that fills with color on hover, via text-stroke.
effects.description: Uses -webkit-text-stroke with a transparent fill for hollow display text, then transitions to a solid fill on hover — a bold editorial/poster treatment.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers via -webkit-text-stroke (works cross-browser despite the prefix).
effects.performance_notes: Static + color transition; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Hollow text can be low-contrast — keep stroke thick enough and the background plain. Provide a solid-fill fallback for very small sizes where strokes get muddy.
effects.ai_usage: Use for large display headings, posters, and section numbers. Set -webkit-text-stroke: WIDTH COLOR and color: transparent for a hollow look; transition color to a solid fill on hover/scroll for emphasis. Keep to big, bold weights — thin/small text loses legibility. Ensure contrast and provide a solid fallback at small sizes.
effects.source: BOLD

effects.title: Shimmer / Shine Text
effects.summary: A bright highlight sweeps across muted text like light catching metal — the classic skeleton/shine loop.
effects.description: Uses a gradient that is mostly the base color with a narrow bright band, clipped to the text, and slides the band across with background-position.
effects.performance_notes: Single element, animates background-position — very cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; animation pauses under prefers-reduced-motion leaving readable text. Keep the base color readable on its own.
variations: Gold/silver palette for a metallic plaque
Slow it down for a luxury feel
effects.ai_usage: Two uses: (1) decorative shiny headings, (2) loading-state text placeholders. Apply .shimmer to text. The trick is a 3-stop gradient (base, shine, base) sized larger than the element so the shine band can travel. For loading skeletons, apply the same technique to a gray block instead of text.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Text Reveal Mask
effects.summary: Lines of text slide up from behind a clipping mask, like film titles.
effects.description: Each line sits in an overflow:hidden wrapper; the inner span is translated from below to its resting position with a staggered delay, producing an elegant editorial reveal.
effects.performance_notes: Animates transform/opacity only — GPU-friendly. Stagger via animation-delay.
effects.accessibility_notes: Text is in the DOM and readable; animation collapses to an instant show under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Add a slight rotation for a flip-up
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero headlines and section intros. Structure: each line is …; the outer clips, the inner animates translateY from --rise to 0. Stagger lines by multiplying --stagger by line index (inline style or :nth-child). For scroll-triggered reveals, add the animating class when the element enters the viewport instead of on load.
effects.source: Designed in motion, revealed line by line.

effects.title: Text Scramble / Decode
effects.summary: Text resolves from random glyph noise into the real words, like a terminal decoding — on load and on hover.
effects.description: Each character cycles through random symbols before locking to its final letter, staggered left to right, producing a 'decrypting' reveal. A favorite for hero words and hover labels on dev/cyber/creative sites.
effects.performance_notes: A single requestAnimationFrame loop mutating textContent for the duration of the reveal, then it stops. Lightweight.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep the final text as the element's accessible name (set it before/after, or use aria-label) so screen readers don't read scrambled glyphs. Under reduced-motion, show final text instantly.
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero words, section labels, and hover micro-copy on dev/cyber/creative sites. Implement a setText(newText) that, per character, randomly shows pool glyphs until a per-char 'settle' frame, then locks the real char; drive it with requestAnimationFrame. Set the real string as aria-label (or render it then animate) so assistive tech reads the word, and skip the animation under prefers-reduced-motion.
effects.source:

effects.title: Typewriter Text
effects.summary: Text types itself out with a blinking caret using the steps() timing function.
effects.description: Animates max-width / width from 0 with steps() so characters appear one glyph at a time, plus a border-right caret that blinks.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. The pure-CSS version needs a known character count and a monospace font.
effects.performance_notes: Animating width triggers layout but on a single short line it is negligible.
effects.accessibility_notes: Full text is present in the DOM immediately (just visually clipped), so screen readers get it. Animation is skipped under prefers-reduced-motion (text shown instantly).

customization.name: --chars
customization.description: Number of characters (set to your text length)

variations: Loop with multiple phrases via JS
Solid block caret instead of bar
Remove caret after typing with a forwards fill
effects.ai_usage: Great for hero taglines and terminal/CLI aesthetics. Pure-CSS version requires: a monospace font, the text wrapped in an inline-block element, ch-based width animation, and --chars set to the exact character count. For dynamic/looping text, prefer a tiny JS loop that swaps textContent. Keep white-space:nowrap and overflow:hidden.
effects.source: Hello world

effects.title: Accordion Expand
effects.summary: A native <details> accordion with a smoothly animated open/close using grid-rows.
effects.description: Builds the accordion on the semantic <details>/<summary> element and animates the panel height with the grid-template-rows 0fr→1fr technique (which animates from auto height cleanly), plus a rotating chevron.
effects.browser_support: Modern browsers; the 0fr→1fr grid animation works in current Chromium/Firefox/Safari. Older browsers simply jump (acceptable).
effects.performance_notes: Animates grid-template-rows; the content wrapper must be overflow:hidden. No JS needed for toggle (native details).
effects.accessibility_notes: Built on <details>/<summary>, so it's keyboard-operable and announced as a disclosure out of the box — no ARIA needed. Honors prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Exclusive accordion (name attribute on <details> in supporting browsers)
effects.ai_usage: Use for FAQs and settings disclosures. Prefer the native <details>/<summary> for free accessibility. To animate the open height, wrap panel content in a div and use display:grid; grid-template-rows:0fr on closed and 1fr on [open], with the wrapper overflow:hidden. Rotate the chevron on [open]. For 'only one open', use the shared name attribute on <details> where supported. Reduced-motion: drop the transition.
effects.source:
What is Style Catalog? ▾

A museum and catalogue of web effects, built for AI agents.

How do I add an effect? ▾

Run npm run new, edit the file, then npm run build.

effects.title: Floating Bob
effects.summary: A gentle up-and-down hover float that makes hero illustrations feel alive.
effects.description: An infinite ease-in-out translateY loop (optionally with a soft shadow that breathes) gives an object a weightless, floating quality.
effects.performance_notes: Transform-only loop — GPU-friendly and cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Ambient motion; disable under prefers-reduced-motion. Avoid on text the user must read precisely.
variations: Add a synced shadow that grows as it rises
Slight rotation for a drifting feel
Different delays for a group of floats
effects.ai_usage: Use on hero illustrations, mascots, icons, and badges to add life. Apply .float; the keyframe eases translateY between 0 and -var(--amp). Give grouped elements different --dur and animation-delay so they don't move in lockstep. Mandatory reduced-motion guard.

effects.title: Modal Fade & Scale
effects.summary: A dialog that fades its backdrop and scales up from 95%, using the native <dialog> element.
effects.description: Uses the semantic <dialog> (with showModal()) for built-in focus trapping and backdrop, animating the panel from a slightly-scaled, transparent state and fading ::backdrop.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transition; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: The native <dialog> with showModal() gives focus trapping, Escape-to-close, and inert background for free — strongly preferred over a div-based modal. Return focus to the trigger on close (handled here).
effects.ai_usage: Use the NATIVE <dialog> for modals — call dialog.showModal() (not just .show()) to get focus trapping, ::backdrop, and Escape handling for free. Animate the panel (opacity + transform: scale) and ::backdrop opacity via an [open] class or the dialog[open] state; manage display with @starting-style or by toggling a class after showModal. Always return focus to the trigger on close. The demo auto-reopens to show the transition.
effects.source:
Native dialog

Focus-trapped, Escape-closable, animated.

effects.title: Morphing Blob
effects.summary: An organic shape slowly morphs by animating border-radius — no SVG needed.
effects.description: Animating the eight-value border-radius syntax between several lopsided states makes a single div ooze between organic blob shapes, popular as decorative hero accents.
effects.performance_notes: Animating border-radius repaints but on a single element it's fine. Add a slow rotation for extra life.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; disable under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Use as an image mask (clip an to the blob)
Add a slow rotate for more motion
effects.ai_usage: Decorative hero accents, avatar frames, or image masks. Apply .blob; the keyframes interpolate the long-form border-radius (h-radii / v-radii) between asymmetric values. Combine a slow rotate to avoid the morph looking like simple pulsing. To mask an image, give the the same border-radius animation or use it as a clip shape.

effects.title: Scroll Reveal Fade-Up
effects.summary: Elements fade and slide up as they enter the viewport, via IntersectionObserver.
effects.description: Items start hidden (opacity 0, translateY); an IntersectionObserver adds an 'in' class when they scroll into view, triggering a CSS transition. The web-standard, dependency-free replacement for scroll libraries.
effects.performance_notes: Far cheaper than scroll-event listeners; the observer fires only at thresholds. Transition animates opacity/transform.
effects.accessibility_notes: Content is in the DOM and accessible regardless of reveal state. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show everything immediately (no transform).

customization.name: threshold
customization.description: How much must be visible to trigger

effects.ai_usage: The standard scroll-entrance for marketing pages. Give items class .reveal (opacity:0; translateY). Create one IntersectionObserver, observe all .reveal nodes, and add .in on intersection (optionally unobserve to fire once). Stagger with transition-delay tied to index. ALWAYS short-circuit to the visible state under prefers-reduced-motion. The demo auto-scrolls so the effect is visible in the catalog frame.
effects.source:
Reveal 1
Reveal 2
Reveal 3

Reveal 5

effects.title: Shake (Attention / Error)
effects.summary: A quick horizontal shake to flag an error or invalid input.
effects.description: A short keyframe wobbles translateX left/right a few times then settles — the universal 'wrong password' / validation-error cue. Demo re-triggers on a timer (and on click).
effects.performance_notes: Single short transform animation; negligible.
effects.accessibility_notes: Motion is brief but should be paired with a text error message and aria-live, not used alone. Reduce-motion users get a static color flash instead of shaking.
variations: Combine with a red border flash
effects.ai_usage: Trigger on form validation failure by toggling a .shake class (remove then re-add on the next frame to restart the animation, or listen for animationend to clean up). Always accompany with a visible, aria-live error message — motion alone is not accessible. Under reduced motion, flash the border/background color instead of translating.
effects.source: Invalid — click or wait to shake

effects.title: Fluid Typography (clamp)
effects.summary: Type that scales smoothly with the viewport using clamp() — no breakpoints.
effects.description: Demonstrates clamp(min, preferred-vw, max) for headings/body so font sizes interpolate fluidly between a minimum and maximum across screen widths, eliminating media-query font jumps.
effects.performance_notes: Pure CSS; recalculated on resize by the engine — free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Include a rem term in the preferred value (e.g. 1rem + 2vw) so text still responds to user zoom/font-size; a pure-vw value can break zoom. Respect the min so text never gets too small.
variations: Fluid spacing/margins with the same technique
Combine with a modular scale for fluid steps
effects.ai_usage: Use to make headings responsive WITHOUT media queries. Pattern: font-size: clamp(MIN, REM + VW, MAX). ALWAYS include a rem term in the middle (e.g. 1rem + 4vw) so the text still scales with user zoom — a pure vw value is an accessibility failure. Pick MIN/MAX from your type scale. Resize the catalog frame / window to see it interpolate.
effects.source:
Resize me, I flow

Fluid type with clamp(2rem, 1rem + 5vw, 4.5rem) — smooth scaling, no breakpoints, still zoomable.

effects.title: Font Pairings
effects.summary: Reference combinations of heading + body fonts using only system font stacks.
effects.description: Shows several tasteful heading/body pairings (serif display + sans body, geometric + humanist, monospace accent) built from cross-platform system font stacks so they need no web-font downloads.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (system font stacks resolve per OS).
effects.performance_notes: System fonts = zero network cost and no layout shift. The fastest possible typography.
effects.accessibility_notes: System stacks respect user/OS font settings. Ensure adequate size and line-height for readability (1.5 for body).
variations: Swap a system stack for a self-hosted web font
Pair weights, not just families, within one superfamily
effects.ai_usage: A reference for pairing fonts WITHOUT web-font downloads. Define --font-head and --font-body as system stacks and apply to headings/body. Principles shown: contrast families (serif heading + sans body), or stay in one family with weight contrast. Prefer system stacks for performance; only add a web font when brand truly requires it (then preload + font-display: swap). Copy a card's two stacks as your tokens.
effects.source:
Editorial Serif

Serif display over a clean sans body — classic, trustworthy, great for long-form reading.

head: Georgia, serif · body: system-ui, sans-serif

Modern Geometric

A bold geometric sans heading paired with a serif body for an unexpected, refined contrast.

head: Trebuchet MS, sans-serif · body: Georgia, serif

Technical Mono

Monospace heading signals a developer/technical product; sans body keeps it readable.

head: ui-monospace, monospace · body: system-ui, sans-serif

effects.title: Interactive Variable Weight
effects.summary: Headline letters thicken toward the cursor — each glyph's weight responds to how close the pointer is, like a spotlight of boldness.
effects.description: Variable-font interactivity is a 2026 typography trend. Each letter is a span; on pointermove, its font-weight (font-variation-settings 'wght') is set by its distance to the cursor, so a wave of weight follows the pointer across the word. Smoothly interpolates with a true variable font.
effects.browser_support: Variable fonts everywhere; smoothest with a loaded variable font. With static system fonts it snaps between available weights (still effective).
effects.performance_notes: One pointermove writing font-variation-settings on each letter span. Fine for headline-length text; avoid on whole paragraphs.
effects.accessibility_notes: The full word stays as text (and as the heading's accessible name); weight is decorative. Pointer-only; disabled under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Animate weight as a looping wave on load
Per-word instead of per-letter for long text
effects.ai_usage: Use on hero words and big numerals where you have a variable font loaded. Split text into per-letter spans; on pointermove, set each letter's font-variation-settings: 'wght' N where N maps inversely to its distance from the cursor (clamped to your min/max). Keep the full word as real text for a11y, load an actual variable font for smooth interpolation, and disable under reduced motion.
effects.source: move your cursor across the word

effects.title: Modular Type Scale
effects.summary: A harmonious heading/body size scale generated from one ratio, as copy-ready tokens.
effects.description: A reference specimen showing a modular scale (each step = previous × ratio, e.g. 1.25 'major third') mapped to --text-xs … --text-4xl tokens, plus a rendered preview of each step.
effects.performance_notes: Variables + static text; free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use rem so user font-size settings scale everything. Keep body text >= 1rem (16px). Maintain a clear size jump between heading levels for hierarchy.

customization.name: --ratio
customization.description: Scale ratio (1.2 minor third, 1.25 major third, 1.333 perfect fourth, 1.618 golden)

variations: Use calc() chains or Sass to derive steps
effects.ai_usage: A copy-paste typographic scale. Choose a --ratio (1.2-1.333 for UI, up to 1.618 for editorial) and derive each token from the base: --text-lg = base*ratio, --text-xl = base*ratio^2, etc. Express in rem so OS/user zoom works. Reference these tokens for all font-size declarations instead of arbitrary px. Keep body >= 16px and ensure adjacent heading levels are visually distinct.
effects.source:
--text-4xlDisplay
--text-3xlHeading 1
--text-2xlHeading 2
--text-xlHeading 3
--text-lgLead paragraph
--text-baseBody text · ratio 1.25 (major third)
--text-smCaption / small print

effects.title: Readable Measure & Rhythm
effects.summary: Reference for line length (measure), line-height, and line-clamp — the rules that make body text readable.
effects.description: A specimen demonstrating the readability fundamentals: a 60-75 character measure via max-width: Nch, a comfortable line-height, and multi-line truncation with -webkit-line-clamp.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers; line-clamp uses -webkit-line-clamp (cross-browser despite the prefix).
effects.performance_notes: Static; free.
effects.accessibility_notes: A 60-75ch measure and ~1.5 line-height materially improve readability for everyone, especially users with dyslexia/low vision. Don't clamp essential content without a way to read the rest.
effects.ai_usage: Apply these readability defaults to all body copy: set the text container max-width to ~60-75ch (the 'measure'); use line-height ~1.5-1.65 for body; for card excerpts, clamp with display:-webkit-box; -webkit-line-clamp:N; -webkit-box-orient:vertical; overflow:hidden. Never let body text run the full width of a wide viewport. Don't clamp content that has no 'read more' path.
effects.source:
max-width: 60ch · line-height: 1.6

A comfortable measure

Lines of 60 to 75 characters are easiest to read: the eye tracks from the end of one line to the start of the next without losing its place. Constrain body text with a ch-based max-width instead of letting it span the whole viewport.

-webkit-line-clamp: 2

This excerpt is clamped to two lines and ends with an ellipsis, which is perfect for card summaries and list previews where space is limited and the full text lives elsewhere.

effects.title: 404 — Page Not Found
effects.summary: A playful 404 page: a glitching gradient '404', a friendly message, a search box, and quick links home — over a subtle dot grid.
effects.description: A memorable error page that turns a dead end into brand personality: an oversized glitch-animated 404, a helpful line of copy, a working-looking search, and shortcut links back into the site, all on a dark dot-grid backdrop.
effects.performance_notes: A CSS glitch (clipped pseudo-layers) + dot-grid background. Tiny. Glitch stops under reduced-motion.
effects.accessibility_notes: Real heading + landmark, the search is a labeled form, links are keyboard-accessible with focus-visible, and the glitch is decorative (the readable 404 stays). Glitch disabled under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: links
customization.description: Quick links back into the site

effects.ai_usage: Use as a custom 404 (or 500/maintenance). Keep it helpful, not just cute: a big readable error number with an optional glitch, one line of copy, a search field, and 3–4 links back into the site (home, docs, contact). Use real landmarks/labels, keep focus-visible, and disable the glitch under prefers-reduced-motion.

effects.title: 90s GeoCities Homepage
effects.summary: A loving 1990s personal homepage: starry tiled background, marquee, visitor counter, webring, and 'under construction'.
effects.description: A nostalgic mid-90s 'personal homepage' — centered table-style column on a tiled starfield, neon headings, a scrolling marquee, a faux hit counter, an 'under construction' banner, a webring nav, and 'best viewed in Netscape'. Modernized to be accessible and dependency-free.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers — recreated without deprecated <marquee>/<blink> tags.
effects.performance_notes: CSS marquee animation only; tiled SVG-data-URI background. No images or JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Homage to a chaotic era but kept usable: real headings/links, the marquee pauses on hover/focus and stops under prefers-reduced-motion, contrast is legible. Use ironically — most of these patterns are anti-patterns today.
variations: Auto-playing MIDI note (don't — but you could)
effects.ai_usage: Use for nostalgia projects, 404 easter eggs, or retro-themed events. Copy the document and edit the name/bio/counter. It deliberately evokes GeoCities but avoids deprecated tags and seizure risks: the marquee is CSS (pauses on hover, stops under reduced-motion). Treat the patterns as a costume, not best practice.

effects.title: Admin Dashboard
effects.summary: An app shell: fixed sidebar nav, topbar, KPI stat cards, a pure-CSS bar chart, and a data table.
effects.description: A static dashboard scaffold using the holy-grail grid (sidebar + topbar + content). Includes KPI cards, a CSS-only bar chart placeholder, and a responsive table — the skeleton for an internal tool or SaaS app.
effects.performance_notes: No JS or chart library; the bar chart is CSS heights. Swap in a real chart lib when wiring live data.
effects.accessibility_notes: Landmarks (aside/nav/header/main), a real <table> with <th scope>, focus-visible on nav items. Charts need text alternatives when populated with real data.
variations: Replace the CSS chart with a real charting lib
effects.ai_usage: Use as the shell for internal tools / SaaS apps. Copy the document; the layout is a CSS grid (sidebar + topbar + main). Replace nav items, KPI numbers, the bar-chart values (set each bar's height), and the table rows. Wire real data by swapping the static table/chart for components, keeping the grid and tokens. Keep the <table> semantics and add aria labels to charts when populated.

effects.title: Anti-Design / Maximalist
effects.summary: Intentional chaos: clashing system fonts, raw borders, overlapping rotated blocks, blinding color, and a marquee — anti-design as a statement.
effects.description: Anti-design (and its maximalist cousin) rejects polish on purpose — default fonts, visible structure, clashing colors, overlap, and 'ugly' energy used deliberately for personality. This page channels it for a zine/creative brand: a chaotic hero, a ransom-note grid, a marquee, and raw form controls — yet still readable and functional.
effects.performance_notes: Mostly system fonts, borders, and one marquee. Extremely light by design.
effects.accessibility_notes: Chaos is visual, not structural: real heading order, sufficient text contrast on each block, focus-visible, and a reduced-motion guard for the marquee and any wobble. Use the aesthetic intentionally — it's not an excuse to skip a11y.
variations: Tone down to one clash color
effects.ai_usage: Use for zines, art projects, music, fashion drops, and brands that want raw personality. The look = default fonts (Times/Courier/Arial mixed), thick black borders, clashing flat colors, slight rotations and overlap, and a marquee. Crucially keep it accessible: real heading order, contrast on each block, focus styles, reduced-motion guard. It's intentional ugliness, not broken markup.

effects.title: Blog Article
effects.summary: A readable long-form article layout: title, byline, cover, prose with a constrained measure, and a footer.
effects.description: An editorial blog-post scaffold tuned for reading: a centered column at a ~68ch measure, comfortable line-height, styled headings/quotes/code, a byline with reading time, and a simple footer. The template version of good typography.
effects.performance_notes: Text-only; instant.
effects.accessibility_notes: Single main heading, logical heading order, ~68ch measure and 1.7 line-height for readability, high contrast, real <article>/<time> semantics.
variations: Add a sticky reading-progress bar (see scroll-progress-bar)
effects.ai_usage: Use for blog posts, docs prose, and articles. Copy the document and replace the title, byline, cover, and body. The key is the --measure (keep 60–75ch) and line-height for readability — don't widen the column. Headings, blockquote, and inline code are pre-styled. Pair with the scroll-progress-bar effect for a reading indicator. Set a real <time datetime>.
effects.source:
The Margin
LatestTopicsAbout

Engineering

Designing for the agent era

By Jordan Lee · May 30, 2026 · 6 min read

The interfaces we build are increasingly read by machines as often as people. That shift rewards structure: predictable markup, explicit metadata, and content that means the same thing whether it’s rendered or parsed.

Structure is the feature

When a document is well-structured, both a screen reader and a language model can navigate it. Headings become an outline; landmarks become a map. The same discipline that aids accessibility aids automation.

Write for the parser you wish you had, and the human you actually have.

Prefer semantic elements, keep a sensible measure like max-width: 68ch, and let typography do the work. The result reads well and machines love it.

Where to start

Pick one template, ship it, and iterate. Momentum beats perfection — especially when redeploys are a single merge away.

© 2026 The Margin · Written by humans, indexed by machines.

effects.title: Restaurant — Interactive Menu
effects.summary: A warm restaurant page with a real, tabbed menu (starters / mains / desserts / drinks), hours, and a reservation form.
effects.description: Unlike a generic landing, this centers the thing diners want: the menu. A warm editorial hero leads into a tabbed menu with dish names, descriptions, dietary dots, and prices, followed by hours/location and a reservation form. Cozy, appetizing, and practical.
effects.performance_notes: A tiny tab toggle + reveals. No images (gradient food tiles). Light.
effects.accessibility_notes: Menu tabs are real buttons with aria-selected and keyboard focus; menu is a semantic list; dietary markers have text labels; reservation uses a labeled form. Reduced-motion safe.
effects.ai_usage: Use for restaurants, cafés, and bars where the menu is the point. Build category tabs as real buttons toggling panels (aria-selected); render each menu category as a semantic list of dish rows (name, description, dietary markers with text labels, price). Include hours/location and a labeled reservation form. Recolor via the warm tokens; replace dishes. Reduced-motion safe.

effects.title: Retro-Futurism — Synthwave Game
effects.summary: An 80s synthwave landing for a game/album: an animated neon perspective grid, chrome-gradient title, sun, and scanlines.
effects.description: Retro-futurism / synthwave is a recurring 2026 aesthetic for games, music, and events — neon, chrome, and that endless perspective grid receding into a gradient sun. This landing has an animated scrolling grid floor, a chrome title with glow, a glassy CTA, a feature row, and CRT scanlines.
effects.performance_notes: The grid is a CSS gradient with a scrolling background-position animation; scanlines are a repeating gradient overlay. No images or libraries.
effects.accessibility_notes: Text glow kept readable; scanlines are subtle and pointer-events:none; grid scroll and glow pulse stop under reduced-motion.
variations: Drop scanlines for a cleaner look
Add a real character/3D render over the grid
effects.ai_usage: Use for games, synth/electronic music, retro events, arcades. The signature is the perspective grid (a linear-gradient grid skewed with transform: rotateX) scrolling toward the viewer, plus a gradient 'sun' and chrome text. Recolor via --neon/--neon2. Replace the title and feature copy. Reduced-motion safe.

customization.name: content regions

The layout is responsive via auto-fit grids; keep the semantic landmarks and heading order. No build step or dependencies required.

effects.title: Skeuomorphic Revival — Music Player
effects.summary: Tactile, realistic UI: a brushed-metal music player with a glossy LCD, embossed buttons, and a chunky knob — skeuomorphism, reborn.
effects.description: Skeuomorphism is making a 2026 comeback as a reaction to flatness — realistic materials rendered purely in CSS. This landing showcases a hi-fi music player 'device': brushed-metal body, inset glossy screen, beveled play/skip buttons, an LED, and a metallic volume knob, beside a product pitch.
effects.performance_notes: All materials are CSS — layered linear/radial gradients and multi-layer box-shadows (inset bevels + drop shadows). No images. A small JS play toggle animates the equalizer.
effects.accessibility_notes: Controls are real buttons with aria-labels/aria-pressed; the screen text is real; focus-visible rings; equalizer bars stop under reduced-motion.
effects.ai_usage: Use for product/hardware brands, audio apps, or nostalgic flair. Materials are pure CSS: brushed metal = layered repeating-linear-gradient + overall linear-gradient; bevels = paired inset light/dark box-shadows; gloss = a top white-to-transparent overlay; the knob = radial gradients with a notch. Keep all controls real <button>s with aria-labels and a reduced-motion guard on the equalizer.

effects.title: Spatial Depth — Parallax Landing
effects.summary: A depth-driven landing: layered elements parallax with the cursor and scroll, creating a sense of 3D space behind the headline.
effects.description: Spatial / 3D-depth design is a 2026 direction enabled by cheap WebGL/CSS transforms. This template fakes it elegantly with pure CSS/JS: multiple z-layers (orbs, cards, grid) that translate at different rates on pointer-move and scroll, so the scene has real parallax depth without a 3D library.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (CSS transforms, pointer events).
effects.performance_notes: One pointermove + one scroll handler writing translate3d on a handful of layers (transform-only, GPU). Parallax disabled under reduced-motion.
effects.accessibility_notes: All motion is decorative; content stays readable and in order with focus-visible. prefers-reduced-motion disables the parallax and reveals.
variations: Add a real product render as the front layer
Tie depth to device orientation on mobile
Swap orbs for floating UI cards
effects.ai_usage: Use for premium product/agency hero sections that want depth without a 3D engine. Give each layer a data-depth; on pointermove translate each by (cursorOffset × depth), and optionally add scrollY × depth for vertical parallax — write translate3d for GPU compositing. Keep the headline a near-zero-depth layer so it stays readable. Disable the handlers under prefers-reduced-motion.

Workshop track as a second schedule
Map/venue embed in the info section

effects.title: Terminal — Developer Tool
effects.summary: A dark, code-forward landing for a CLI/dev tool: a faux terminal with a typing animation, monospace UI, an install command, and a code sample.
effects.description: The developer-tool aesthetic — monospace, dark, terminal-first. This landing opens with a working-looking terminal window that types its commands out, a one-line install with a copy button, a feature grid in code-comment style, and a syntax-tinted snippet. Built for CLIs, APIs, and infra products.
effects.performance_notes: A small typewriter loop (setTimeout) + copy-to-clipboard + reveals. No images or libraries.
effects.accessibility_notes: Terminal text is real text; the typing is decorative and the full command is present for screen readers. Copy button has a label and live status. Reduced-motion shows the command instantly.
variations: Replace typing with a static log
effects.ai_usage: Use for CLIs, SDKs, APIs, and infra/devtool products. The hero is a styled terminal window; a tiny typewriter loop types the LINES array (full text is also in the DOM for a11y). The install bar copies to clipboard. Recolor via --accent/--green. Replace LINES, the install command, features, and the code sample. Reduced-motion shows commands instantly.

effects.title: Testimonial Wall — Social Proof
effects.summary: A social-proof page: a headline stat, a logo strip, and a masonry wall of testimonial cards with ratings, avatars, and a featured quote.
effects.description: The dedicated proof/wall-of-love page — an aggregate rating headline, trusted-by logos, one large featured testimonial, and a masonry grid of varied-length quote cards with star ratings and author chips. Built to convert skeptics.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (CSS columns for masonry).
effects.performance_notes: CSS multi-column masonry + reveals. No images (avatar gradients). Light.
effects.accessibility_notes: Testimonials are <figure>/<blockquote> with <figcaption>; star ratings include text (e.g. '5 out of 5'); logos are labeled; focus-visible. Reduced-motion safe.
effects.ai_usage: Use as a standalone proof page or a section. Lead with an aggregate rating + trusted-by logos, then a masonry wall of testimonial <figure>s (blockquote + figcaption with avatar/name/role) and star ratings that include text alternatives. Use CSS columns for the masonry, keep it real semantic quotes, and recolor via --accent.

effects.title: Waitlist with Referrals
effects.summary: A beta waitlist: join with your email, then see your queue position and a referral link to jump the line — the viral pre-launch loop.
effects.description: A pre-launch growth page: a focused signup that, on submit, reveals the user's place in line, a shareable referral link with a copy button, and 'invite to move up' incentives. Animated gradient backdrop, single-column focus.
effects.performance_notes: An animated gradient + a form that swaps to a success state and copies a link. No backend (wire your own).
effects.accessibility_notes: Labeled email form, success announced via aria-live, copy button labeled with status, focus moves to the result. Gradient animation stops under reduced-motion.
effects.ai_usage: Use for beta/pre-launch growth. Capture email, then reveal queue position + a unique referral link (copy button) and 'invite N friends to move up' perks. Wire submit + referral params to your backend; announce the result via aria-live, move focus to it, and pause the gradient under reduced motion. The viral loop is the point — make sharing one tap.

effects.title: Web 2.0 Glossy (2000s)
effects.summary: A mid-2000s 'Web 2.0' homepage: glossy gradients, reflective buttons, a beta badge, and rounded boxes.
effects.description: A nostalgic but accurate Web 2.0 layout — big friendly headline, glossy reflective CTA, a 'BETA' star badge, soft gradients, rounded panels with subtle shadows, and a tag-cloud footer. The Helvetica-and-shine era.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (recreated with modern CSS gradients).
effects.performance_notes: Pure CSS gloss (layered gradients + box-shadows); no images.
effects.accessibility_notes: Despite the era's aesthetic, this uses semantic markup, real buttons, and adequate contrast. Decorative shine is CSS only.
variations: Add a faux 'feed' box with rounded rows
effects.ai_usage: Use for nostalgic/retro projects or anniversary pages evoking 2005–2008. The 'gloss' is a top-half white-to-transparent gradient overlaid on a colored gradient; reflective buttons stack two gradients. Recolor via --sky/--lime. Keep it tongue-in-cheek. Modern semantics/contrast are preserved so it stays usable.
